chunks.text: The Hopper-Dean Foundation has awarded $2 million over two years to UC Berkeley’s Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences (EECS) in support of diversity initiatives in computer science. The new funds build upon a generous $1 million gift from the foundation in 2016, which included support of pilot diversity programs developed in the department. Jeff Bokor, chair of the EECS Department, expressed gratitude for the increased support, noting it acknowledges the department's progress in diversity efforts.

Bokor and John Canny, chair of the Computer Science Division, credit the initial Hopper-Dean Foundation gift with the department’s progress in addressing the underrepresentation of women and minority students. Berkeley’s computer science population has seen marked change: graduating women in CS and EECS majors increased by 47%, underrepresented minority students by 43% in the last two years. CS 61A, the introductory course, saw a 52% increase in women over four years, now enrolling 1,145 women out of 2,774 students.

At the graduate level, diversity improvements are even more significant. In fall 2011, only 81 women were enrolled in EECS graduate programs, representing 14.5% of all graduate students. Since then, the number of female students has more than doubled, with women now representing 22.5% of the graduate student population. Underrepresented minority graduate students increased from 27 in fall 2011 to 41 in fall 2018, a growth of over 50%. Challenges remain, but the department aims to build on this momentum with Hopper-Dean Foundation support.

Bokor and Canny anticipate that diversity efforts will benefit thousands of middle school and high school students nationwide, as well as undergraduate and graduate students at UC Berkeley. Canny noted that UC Berkeley EECS is the largest supplier of top-tier talent to the US IT industry, granting over 1,280 undergraduate degrees last year. He expects strides in diversity, equity, and inclusion to influence industry beyond the department and college.

The new gift from the Hopper-Dean Foundation will fund a comprehensive suite of programs supporting students throughout their careers. Initiatives include: The Beauty and Joy of Computing (BJC) for middle and high school students, an AP CS Principles course and outreach effort; CS Kickstart for first-year undergraduates, a week-long introductory seminar for women; and CS Mentors (CSM) for first and second-year undergraduates, a near-peer tutoring program serving 1,500 students weekly in fall 2018.

Additional programs include: CS Scholars for first and second-year undergraduates from disadvantaged backgrounds, providing cohort labs and resources; EECS Graduate Fellows for first-year Ph.D. students, improving retention with early research engagement; Teaching at Scale for second and third-year undergraduates, focusing on adaptive teaching methods; and Transfer-To-Excellence (TTE) for community college students, offering summer research experiences to encourage transfer to UC.

Canny acknowledged the staff, faculty, student instructors, and volunteers for their contributions to the programs, which enhance campus experience and promote an inclusive, welcoming, and diverse community at Berkeley. The article concludes with special thanks to various individuals involved in the initiatives.
